1: The angelic guide brings Ezekiel into the nave (היכל, the Holy Place) and measures its entrance posts. Jesus identifies himself as the true temple: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up' (John 2:19-21). The nave that Ezekiel enters by precise measurement is the sanctuary whose greater fulfillment is Christ's body — every dimension pointing toward the one in whom 'the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9).
2: The nave door opening is 40 cubits long and 20 cubits wide. Forty is the dimension of the Holy Place and the number of probationary testing: forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2), forty years of desert wandering. Christ endured the forty-day wilderness ordeal and so fills the Holy Place with an obedience that Israel's desert generation could never achieve — entering not a sanctuary made with hands but heaven itself (Hebrews 9:24).
3: The inner room (the Most Holy Place, קדש הקדשים) has a six-cubit-wide doorway, seven cubits tall. Hebrews 9:7 notes that only the high priest entered the Most Holy Place once a year, 'not without taking blood.' Christ entered 'the greater and more perfect tabernacle not made with hands' by his own blood, securing an eternal redemption (Hebrews 9:11-12) — the restrictive door of the Most Holy Place flung wide by his sacrifice.
4: The Most Holy Place measured twenty cubits square: 'This is the Most Holy Place.' A twenty-by-twenty square plan mirrors the cubic perfection of the heavenly city: the new Jerusalem is 12,000 stadia in length, width, and height — a colossal cube (Revelation 21:16). The shape of the Most Holy Place is the shape of the whole new creation, because Christ's indwelling presence makes every inch of the new cosmos holy to its core.
5: The wall of the house and the side chambers built against it. The Hebrew for side chamber (צלע, tsela) is the same word used for Adam's 'rib' from which Eve was formed (Genesis 2:21-22). The church is built from the side of the second Adam: John 19:34 records water and blood flowing from Christ's pierced side, and patristic reading sees in this the sacraments (baptism and Eucharist) — the birth of the bride of Christ from his wounded side.
6: The side chambers built in three stories. The threefold vertical ordering of the temple (forecourt, holy place, most holy place) mirrors the threefold structure of all access to God: the outer court open to all Israel, the inner court for priests, the Holy of Holies for the high priest alone. In Christ all three levels of the approach to God are fulfilled and democratized: 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus' (Hebrews 10:19) — all believers now hold high-priestly access.
7: The winding staircase ascending through the three stories. The ascending structure of the temple connects to Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12), which Jesus applies to himself: 'You will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man' (John 1:51). Christ is the winding way of ascent from the ground floor of human life to the highest floor of divine presence.
8: The platform/foundation of the side chambers measured a full reed of six cubits. Paul declares: 'No one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ' (1 Corinthians 3:11). Every chamber in the temple complex rests on a foundation of divine precision — the architectural image of the church built on and secure in the one foundation who is Christ.
9: The outer wall of the side chambers, five cubits thick. Five is associated with grace and Torah: the five books of Moses, the fivefold ministry (Ephesians 4:11). The five-cubit outer wall serves as the protective boundary of the holy side chambers — the law as the outer protective structure that guards the sanctuary of Christ's body, now fulfilled in him who said 'I have not come to abolish the law but to fulfill it' (Matthew 5:17).
10: A twenty-cubit gap between the temple chambers and the priests' chambers on every side. Twenty cubits is the width of the Most Holy Place. This maintained distance echoes the reverential space preserved around the divine presence — the holy distance that becomes free approach in Christ. Hebrews 4:16: 'Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace.' The gap is bridged not by removing holiness but by the great high priest who stands within the gap for us.
11: Doors of the side chambers opening north and south. The north-south orientation — the two remaining cardinal directions after east (the glory's entrance) and west (the separate building) — suggests that access to the rooms of God's temple community opens in all directions. The priestly community's doors face universally, as the church's mission: 'make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19), the chambers of Christ's body open on every side.
12: The separate building (גִּזְרָה) at the western end, facing away from the east. The west is the direction of the nations who have not yet turned toward the eastern glory. This building's presence in the template complex anticipates the inclusion of those yet outside: Ephesians 2:13 — 'But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.' The western separate space becomes integrated into the holy district in the new Jerusalem, where there is no more separation between sacred and profane.
13: Total length of the house: 100 cubits. A hundred is the number of completion in Scripture: Abraham's son born at 100 years (Genesis 21:5), the parable of the hundredfold harvest (Mark 4:8), the ninety-nine left to seek the one lost (Luke 15:4). The temple at 100 cubits reaches its full measure. Paul prays that believers be 'filled with all the fullness of God' (Ephesians 3:19) — the complete measure of the temple met in the stature of Christ.
14: The east face of the temple: 100 cubits wide. The east face receives the returning glory (Ezekiel 43). The east orientation of the temple's full extent is the face the risen Christ turns toward the world at the dawn of resurrection: he who 'came from the east' (Matthew 24:27) and through whom 'the glory of the LORD filled the house' (Ezekiel 43:5) displays himself in all his full measure to the watching creation.
15: The length of the building behind the temple and its galleries. The complete measurement of all the architectural elements. Christ's salvation is not partial or provisional — it accounts for every dimension of the human need for God. John 19:30: 'It is finished.' The entire floor plan is complete; not a single cubit is missing from the atoning work of Christ.
16: The inner decorations: latticed windows, galleries on three sides. The latticed windows admit light without full exposure — they are windows of revelation, seeing without being overwhelmed. Jesus is the light of the world (John 8:12), but the latticed window of Scripture mediates his glory so that we can perceive it without being consumed. The three-sided galleries = the threefold witness: Father, Son, and Spirit (1 John 5:7-8).
17: Measured above and around the inner house. The divine dimensions cover every surface, interior and exterior. Christ's salvation is comprehensive: there is no dimension of human life or cosmic reality that stands outside the scope of his redemptive measurement. Ephesians 3:18: 'the breadth and length and height and depth' of the love of Christ surpasses all measurement while being measurable by the Spirit.
18: The interior walls are covered with alternating cherubim and palm trees. The cherubim (Genesis 3:24) were placed to guard the way to the tree of life after the fall; here they alternate with palm branches inside the restored sanctuary — suggesting their guardianship role is now transformed to celebratory presence. Revelation 2:7: 'To the one who conquers I will grant to eat of the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God.' Christ's victory gives access to the tree the cherubim once guarded.
19: Each cherub has two faces: the face of a man and the face of a young lion. The dual-faced cherubim are the Christological images of the temple's interior: the face of a man points to the Son of Man (Daniel 7:13; John 5:27), the incarnate mediator who is 'bone of our bone'; the face of a lion points to the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5), the conquering King. Christ's two natures — fully human, fully royal-divine — are declared on the walls of his sanctuary. The palms between them are the branches of his triumphal entry (John 12:13) and the palms of his redeemed (Revelation 7:9).
20: From the floor to above the doorway, cherubim and palm trees covered the nave wall. Floor to ceiling, entrance to interior — the entire space of Christ's sanctuary declares his dual identity and his victorious welcome. The sanctuary itself is a visual sermon: everywhere you look within the body of Christ you see the Lion-Man who entered Jerusalem to palm-branches and who now reigns. The church's whole interior life is shaped by this double vision of Christ.
21: The doorposts of the nave were square (foursquare). Square doorposts signal perfect stability and symmetry — the fourfold completeness that runs through Scripture (four directions, four creatures, fourfold Gospel, four-square city). The entrance into the holy place through Christ is perfectly balanced: neither too narrow for sinners nor too wide for holiness. He is 'the door' (John 10:9) whose foursquare frame admits the lost and maintains the sacred.
22: The wooden altar, three cubits high and two cubits square: 'This is the table that stands before the LORD.' The altar is identified as the table — the place of the bread of presence. Three cubits high (the three days of resurrection), two cubits square (the two natures of Christ), and made of wood (the material of the cross). Paul writes: 'We have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat' (Hebrews 13:10). The wooden table-altar before the LORD is the Eucharistic table: Christ who is the altar is also the bread, the one sacrifice and the holy food, offered and shared in the same act.
23: Double doors for the nave and for the inner sanctuary. The doubled doors suggest paired access points into God's presence. Hebrews 10:19-20 speaks of 'the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh.' The two-ply door — outer and inner — corresponds to the outer sacrifice at the cross (the visible, public act of atonement) and the inner intercession in the heavenly sanctuary (the ongoing high-priestly work within the veil).
24: Each door had two swinging leaves. Four leaves total for the two doors — folding open to create full access. Isaiah 45:1 speaks of doors opened before Cyrus; Revelation 3:8 promises Philadelphia 'an open door.' The swinging leaves of the inner sanctuary's double doors fold fully back, removing every barrier, when Christ's atonement is applied. Ephesians 2:18: 'through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father' — both leaves open wide.
25: Cherubim and palm trees decorated the doors of the nave, as on the walls. The door bears the same imagery as the interior — Christ who is the door (John 10:9) carries on his own person the marks of the sanctuary's décor: the human-lion dual nature and the palms of his victory. The entrance point and the interior are continuous; there is no bait-and-switch from an attractive door to a different interior. What you see at the door — the Lion-Man with palms — is what fills the entire house.
26: Latticed windows and palm trees on both sides of the porch. The porch (אולם, ulam) is the outer face of the temple, the threshold where approach meets arrival. Latticed windows admit selective light; palm trees announce victory; thick beams provide support on either side. The approach to Christ combines revelation (windows), triumph (palms), and strength (beams). Those who draw near find that the structure holding them as they enter is the very strength of Christ who 'is the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8).
